sections.name: preamble
sections.text:
University College Hospital

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery

Clinic Date: 20 November 2021

Dr Susan
University College Hospital
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, London WC1N 3BG

Dear Dr Susan,

William Harris, DOB: 15-02-1965, Hospital No: H563829 NHS No. 9273645810
10 Meadow Park, London, EC1V 9BT

Summary/Diagnoses: Focal epilepsy with impaired awareness; history of traumatic brain injury; travel-related anxiety; osteoporosis; former alcohol dependence (in sustained remission); hypertension; hyperlipidaemia. Currently residing in supported accommodation. Smoker.

Medication: Levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily; Carbamazepine (Tegretol Retard) 400 mg twice daily; Topiramate 75 mg morning and 100 mg evening; Atorvastatin 20 mg once daily; Lansoprazole 30 mg once daily; Bisoprolol 5 mg once daily; Ramipril 5 mg once daily; Thiamine 100 mg once daily; Vitamin B compound strong one tablet daily; Calcium/Vitamin D supplements; Carbocisteine 750 mg twice daily; Emollients as required.

I reviewed Bill in clinic today, accompanied by his key worker from the residence. He remains engaged with routines and reports improved appetite. He uses a single-point stick outdoors and is independent indoors. No recent falls. Sleep has improved since a change to a quieter room. He continues to abstain from alcohol and attends weekly wellbeing sessions.

Focal epilepsy: Bill describes stereotyped focal impaired-awareness episodes featuring a rising epigastric sensation, brief arrest of speech, right facial pulling, and post-event confusion for several minutes. Importantly, he and staff document 5 or 7 focal onset seizures in three weeks during a recent period that included an episode while travelling by air. He links this cluster to marked anticipatory and in-flight anxiety, sleep disruption the night before travel, and missed hydration. Outside that three‑week window, there have been no further episodes for the last six weeks. There has been no secondary generalisation, tongue biting, or injuries. No clear intercurrent infection. Medication adherence is generally good; one evening dose of levetiracetam may have been late on the day of outbound travel.

Travel context: Bill acknowledges travel anxiety, particularly around security checks and take-off. He is open to structured preparation and timing medication around flight schedules. No panic attacks outside travel.

Examination: Afebrile, BP 132/78 mmHg, pulse 66 bpm regular. Neurological exam unchanged: mild increased tone right upper limb, subtle right pronator drift, gait slightly wide-based but safe with stick. No focal tenderness; feet intact, no callosities today. Cognition stable to brief screening; orientation preserved.

sections.name: plan
sections.text:
- Diagnosis reaffirmed as focal epilepsy. Maintain current anti-seizure regimen without dose change, given six seizure-free weeks following the travel-related cluster.
- Provide a written travel plan: ensure adherence with doses timed to flight schedule, maintain sleep the night before, regular hydration, and avoidance of excess caffeine. Airport letter supplied to carry medicines in hand luggage.
- Offer brief anxiety management strategies and signpost to primary care for short-term psychological support focused on travel anxiety.
- Safety advice reinforced: no swimming unsupervised, avoid heights, and comply with DVLA guidance—he is not driving.
- Bloods: request U&E, LFT, FBC, vitamin D, and carbamazepine level via practice in the next two weeks.
- Bone health: continue calcium/vitamin D; DEXA recheck due next year.
- Follow-up: neurology clinic in 6 months, sooner if further clusters or any generalised tonic-clonic seizure.

Yours sincerely,

sections.name: preamble
sections.text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Sarah Coleman 
Consultant Neurologist 
Centre for Epilepsy 
King’s College Hospital, Denmark Hill, London SE5 9RS 

Dear Dr Sarah Coleman, 

Alex Turner, DOB: 22-08-1992, Hospital No: K847392 NHS No. 1039485726 
Flat 12, 4 Brookfield Road, London, N16 7HF 

Diagnosis: 

Recurrent unprovoked seizures; phenotype under ongoing evaluation

Aetiology: 

No clear structural, metabolic, or genetic cause identified to date

Other Medical Problems: 

Generalised anxiety symptoms under community psychological support; mild iron-deficiency anaemia treated with oral ferrous fumarate

Management Plan: 

Continue current anti-seizure medicines: Levetiracetam 1,000 mg twice daily and Lacosamide 100 mg twice daily. Maintain single-brand supply for both. Arrange serum levels and U&Es/LFTs prior to next review. Safety advice reinforced around nocturnal events and supervision overnight when feasible. Sleep hygiene and regular routines encouraged. Continue seizure and mood diary. Review in three months, sooner if escalation occurs. 

History and Progress: 

Since the last appointment, the patient reports three nocturnal clusters this month; each ~4 - 5 events. These are brief, stereotyped episodes occurring typically between 02:00 and 04:00, with rapid post-event reorientation. No clear daytime events were reported. The patient notes prodromal mood change and irritability for 12–24 hours prior to some clusters and has been tracking this in a diary, which shows a temporal association with sleep deprivation and increased work-related stress. No new head injuries, intercurrent infections, or alcohol binges reported. 

Medication Tolerability and Past Treatments: 

Previously trialled Carbamazepine (dizziness and diplopia at therapeutic doses) and Topiramate (word-finding difficulties and paraesthesia), both discontinued. Currently tolerating Levetiracetam and Lacosamide with manageable irritability that appears to fluctuate with sleep and stress rather than dose changes. No rash, hyponatraemia symptoms, or weight loss reported. Adherence reported as good; pharmacy records corroborate on monthly collection.

sections.name: investigations
sections.text:
Prior MRI brain (2024) unremarkable. Routine EEG (2024) non-diagnostic. Ambulatory home EEG has been requested to better capture nocturnal events and correlate with the diary; awaiting scheduling. Baseline bloods (June 2025) normal apart from mild iron deficiency now treated. 

Lifestyle and Safety: 

We discussed SUDEP and general safety; the patient uses a phone-based seizure alert app at night and has arranged for a family member to check in when clusters occur. Driving remains not applicable as the patient does not hold a licence. Work arrangements have been modified to avoid night shifts and mitigate sleep loss.

Plan Summary: 

- Continue Levetiracetam 1,000 mg BD and Lacosamide 100 mg BD (single-brand). 
- Pre-clinic bloods: FBC, U&Es, LFTs, Levetiracetam and Lacosamide levels. 
- Proceed with ambulatory EEG to characterise nocturnal clusters. 
- Maintain detailed seizure and mood/sleep diary; bring to next review. 
- Review in three months in clinic; earlier contact if frequency increases or injuries occur. 

Yours sincerely

sections.name: body
sections.text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE

Clinic Date: 11 December 2015 

Dr Anita Shah
Riverside Medical Practice

12 Riverside Way, London, SE1 9ZZ
Dear Dr Anita Shah
Michael Carter, DOB: 21-11-1982, Hospital No: M827461 NHS No. 8845123367
Flat 4, 29 Millstream Court, London, SE16 3LP 

I reviewed the above patient in the Neurology Clinic today. Since his last appointment there has been a notable deterioration in his condition. He reports recent job loss from his banking role, with associated stress and disrupted sleep patterns over several weeks. In the 24 hours prior to clinic he experienced multiple seizures in past day, described by him and his partner as brief episodes of loss of awareness with post-event confusion and marked fatigue. He also reports several near-miss episodes over the preceding week, including sudden staring and unresponsiveness lasting under a minute, followed by headaches and irritability.

He is currently taking Levetiracetam 1 g b.d. and Clobazam 10 mg nocte, both of which he states he has taken regularly. He denies missed doses but admits that sleep fragmentation and early waking have worsened his daytime somnolence and concentration. He also notes increased anxiety since becoming unemployed, with occasional palpitations and a sense of impending events before the episodes. There have been no clear triggers such as intercurrent infection or alcohol excess, though caffeine intake has risen. No injuries were reported, but there is concern from family regarding safety at home given the recent escalation.

On examination today he was alert and oriented with normal speech and cranial nerves. Motor and coordination were intact; no focal neurological deficits were identified. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations were unremarkable. BP in clinic was mildly elevated, likely stress-related.

Given the clinical picture and the clear pattern of worsening events temporally associated with stress and sleep disruption, I have advised close observation and prompt contact if events cluster or if any convulsive episodes occur. I have arranged urgent ambulatory EEG within the next 1–2 weeks and will request updated serum drug levels and routine bloods (U&E, LFT, FBC). He has been provided seizure first-aid guidance for his partner and we discussed practical risk mitigation at home. He is aware of the DVLA guidance and understands he should not drive. I will review him in an expedited follow-up once the ambulatory EEG is completed.

Yours sincerely

KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE

Dr Anita Shah

12 Riverside Way, London, SE1 9ZZ
Dear Dr Anita Shah
Michael Carter, DOB: 21-11-1982, Hospital No: M827461 NHS No. 8845123367
Flat 4, 29 Millstream Court, London, SE16 3LP 

I reviewed the above patient in the Neurology Clinic today. Since his last appointment there has been a notable deterioration in his condition. He reports recent job loss from his banking role, with associated stress and disrupted sleep patterns over several weeks. In the 24 hours prior to clinic he experienced multiple seizures in past day, described by him and his partner as brief episodes of loss of awareness with post-event confusion and marked fatigue. He also reports several near-miss episodes over the preceding week, including sudden staring and unresponsiveness lasting under a minute, followed by headaches and irritability.

He is currently taking Levetiracetam 1 g b.d. and Clobazam 10 mg nocte, both of which he states he has taken regularly. He denies missed doses but admits that sleep fragmentation and early waking have worsened his daytime somnolence and concentration. He also notes increased anxiety since becoming unemployed, with occasional palpitations and a sense of impending events before the episodes. There have been no clear triggers such as intercurrent infection or alcohol excess, though caffeine intake has risen. No injuries were reported, but there is concern from family regarding safety at home given the recent escalation.

On examination today he was alert and oriented with normal speech and cranial nerves. Motor and coordination were intact; no focal neurological deficits were identified. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations were unremarkable. BP in clinic was mildly elevated, likely stress-related.

Given the clinical picture and the clear pattern of worsening events temporally associated with stress and sleep disruption, I have advised close observation and prompt contact if events cluster or if any convulsive episodes occur. I have arranged urgent ambulatory EEG within the next 1–2 weeks and will request updated serum drug levels and routine bloods (U&E, LFT, FBC). He has been provided seizure first-aid guidance for his partner and we discussed practical risk mitigation at home. He is aware of the DVLA guidance and understands he should not drive. I will review him in an expedited follow-up once the ambulatory EEG is completed.

Yours sincerely

sections.name: preamble
sections.text:
Department of Neurology

Clinic Date: 15 January 2019

Dr Eleanor Hughes
St Cuthbert Neurology Centre
Neurology Outpatients, 12 Morley Lane, Leeds LS2 8AB

Dear Dr Eleanor Hughes,

Michael Turner, DOB: 03-02-1991, Hospital No: L937520 NHS No. 9054312768
22 Brookside Close, Leeds, LS7 3HT

Diagnoses:

Focal epilepsy with impaired awareness seizures; prior MRI unremarkable; interictal EEG previously normal.

Current Management:

Ketogenic/modified diet (supervised by an external specialist dietetic team); diet details recorded in notes. No regular anti-seizure medicines at present.

sections.name: plan
sections.text:
Continue current dietary therapy with ongoing external dietetic supervision and ensure copies of dietary reviews are shared with our service. Safety advice reinforced. Routine bloods (lipid profile, liver function, bicarbonate) via GP every 6 months while on diet. Review in neurology clinic in 12 months, or sooner if events recur or dietary intolerance emerges.

I reviewed Michael Turner in clinic today. He reports a history consistent with focal epilepsy, characterised previously by brief episodes of behavioural arrest with automatisms and post‑event confusion lasting a few minutes. He has been following a clinician‑supervised ketogenic/modified diet for several months; the dietary protocol and ratios have been documented in the medical record. Since adopting the diet, he describes that seizure occurrences have not been happening, and there have been no witnessed episodes by family or colleagues. He denies new neurological symptoms. Sleep and hydration are reported as stable. He is not currently taking lamotrigine or other anti‑seizure medicines.

On examination today he was well, with normal neurological findings. We discussed ongoing safety measures, driving regulations where applicable, and the importance of maintaining meticulous dietary adherence and regular monitoring while on this therapy. I have asked that the external dietetic team continue to supervise macronutrient ratios and provide periodic summaries for our records. Should any breakthrough focal events occur, we would reconsider pharmacological options and repeat EEG.

I have arranged follow‑up in 12 months. He may contact the clinic earlier if there is any change in seizure pattern, adverse effects from the diet, or other concerns.

With best wishes,

sections.name: body
sections.text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 22 November 2024 

Dr Priya Nair
Consultant Neurologist 
King's College Hospital 
Department of Neurosciences, King's College Hospital, London SE5 9RS 

Dear Dr Priya Nair
Consultant Neurologist, 

Daniel Fraser, DOB: 14-03-1986, Hospital No: KCH928374 NHS No. 9432175609 
12 Brookfield Close, London, SE15 3QA

Thank you for asking me to assess this patient, who attended today with his partner. He reports intermittent episodes over several years characterised by a brief prodrome of tightness across the chest, a rising internal "surge", and an abrupt loss of awareness witnessed by his partner. On most occasions there is bilateral hand fumbling and a brief vocalisation. He does not recall the episodes and is usually confused for 10–15 minutes afterwards, occasionally with a dull bifrontal headache. He denies tongue biting or incontinence. There is no clear motor convulsion described. He keeps a diary and, based on this, the episodes are now occurring abs monthly, with intervening weeks free of events. He links recent clustering to periods of poor sleep and heavy workload.

Of note, he acknowledges high caffeine intake. He typically consumes four to five large coffees by midday and often an energy drink during late meetings. He and his partner have noticed that on days with very high caffeine use and reduced sleep, he feels more jittery and has experienced the prodromal "surge" without progression on two occasions. He has reduced alcohol substantially and reports no recreational drug use. He does not drive. There is no family history of similar episodes. He had a normal birth and development and no history of significant head injury or CNS infection. His medications include ramipril for hypertension and vitamin D. He is not on any antiseizure medication currently.

Previous investigations: routine EEG one year ago reportedly normal; MRI brain without contrast five years ago for headaches was described as normal. I have not yet reviewed the original images or tracings. Cardiovascular review is unremarkable; no syncope, palpitations, or exertional chest pain. Neurological examination today was normal, including cranial nerves, motor, coordination, and gait. Mood appears generally stable; he describes situational anxiety around work deadlines but no persistent low mood, psychotic features, or panic attacks. Sleep is fragmented during busy periods (often <6 hours).

Overall, there are paroxysmal episodes with impaired awareness and post-event confusion. The semiology is not sufficiently specific to assign a definitive classification at this stage. Given the current pattern abs monthly, further characterisation is required to guide management and risk counselling. I have discussed prolonged EEG monitoring with home ambulatory recording as a first step, followed by inpatient video-EEG if required. He is agreeable to this plan. I will also arrange an MRI brain with epilepsy protocol to ensure high-resolution temporal lobe coverage, and I have requested thyroid function and metabolic profile via his GP to exclude contributory systemic factors.

In discussion, he independently raised concern about his caffeine habits. He will keep a simple log of daily caffeine amounts and sleep duration alongside his existing event diary so that we can correlate potential precipitants with episodes. We have also provided standard seizure first-aid information to his partner. No treatment changes were initiated today pending further data.

I will review him in clinic after the ambulatory EEG and MRI have been completed and will update you with the outcomes. Please let me know if there are interim concerns or if additional information from prior investigations becomes available.

Yours sincerely
